title: Non-commutative Cartier Isomorphism and Quantum Cohomology
speaker: Daniel Pomerleano
abstract:
Kaledin established a Cartier isomorphism for cyclic homology of dg-categories over fields of characteristic p, generalizing a classical construction in algebraic geometry. In joint work with Paul Seidel, we showed that this isomorphism and related results imply concrete statements about the structure of quantum connections on monotone symplectic manifolds (both in characteristic p and characteristic zero).
I will explain these results and, if time permits, I will also describe some open questions concerning the enumerative interpretation of the Cartier isomorphism as well as connections to quantum Steenrod operations.

proof_stages.label: Quantum connection and algebraic monodromy benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by defining the monotone symplectic quantum connection given by quantum multiplication by c1, then recalls the classical Gauss-Manin picture for smooth proper algebraic families, where local monodromies are quasi-unipotent with bounded Jordan blocks and regular singularities.

proof_stages.label: Exponential-type theorem and Jordan-block refinements
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the quantum connection typically has an irregular second-order pole at q=infinity, states the Seidel-Pomerleano result that under a smooth symplectic anticanonical divisor with Weinstein complement this singularity is unramified exponential type with quasi-unipotent regularized monodromy, and compares the resulting dimension bounds with newer bounds coming from Chen's quantum-Steenrod method.

proof_stages.label: Laplace-transform mirror model
proof_stages.summary: To motivate the symplectic proof, the talk reviews the mirror algebraic model in which a twisted de Rham/Gauss-Manin D-module for a proper algebraic function is Laplace transformed, turning a regular-singular object into a connection that is regular at q=0 and exponential type at q=infinity.

proof_stages.label: q-deformed symplectic cohomology of the divisor complement
proof_stages.summary: The symplectic analogue of the Gauss-Manin D-module is constructed as a q-deformation of symplectic cohomology for M\D, using a telescope Floer model and counting curves with extra marked points meeting the divisor, weighted by powers of q and accompanied by slope increases.

proof_stages.label: PSS-type identification and localization to quantum cohomology
proof_stages.summary: A deformed PSS-type map identifies the deformed symplectic cohomology with H*(M)[q] plus formal tangency contributions from H*(D), and after inverting q these divisor contributions disappear, giving the vector space underlying quantum cohomology.

proof_stages.label: S1-equivariant Gauss-Manin connection equals the quantum connection
proof_stages.summary: The construction is upgraded to S1-equivariant symplectic cohomology, where a canonical Hamiltonian Floer connection exists, and after inverting q and the equivariant variable u this connection is identified with the quantum connection.

proof_stages.label: p-curvature criterion and noncommutative Cartier input
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy invokes Katz's p-curvature criterion, where nilpotent p-curvature over reductions modulo p implies regular singularities, quasi-unipotent monodromy, and Jordan-block bounds, together with Kaledin--Petrov--Vaintrob--Volgodsky's categorical version for periodic cyclic homology of smooth proper dg categories.

proof_stages.label: Wrapped-Fukaya localization and quantum-Steenrod alternative
proof_stages.summary: The categorical theorem is applied to the wrapped Fukaya category of M\D made linear over a curve by the divisor-intersection class and localized by the minimal polynomial of quantum multiplication, while the Q&A explains the localization analogy and then outlines Chen's alternative proof via equality, up to nilpotents or exactly in later work, between p-curvature and quantum Steenrod operations.

examples.name: Algebraic Fano varieties satisfying the divisor hypothesis
examples.note: The speaker notes that the known monotone examples usually come from algebraic Fano varieties, where the smooth anticanonical divisor and Weinstein-complement assumptions are automatically available, illustrating that the theorem's hypotheses cover many familiar cases.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only cites algebraic Fano varieties as the source of most known examples where the Weinstein-complement condition is automatic

examples.name: Semisimple quantum multiplication
examples.note: As an illustrative consequence of the quantum-Steenrod/minimal-polynomial bound, if quantum multiplication by c1 is semisimple then the regularized monodromies have finite order.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the Jordan-block bound to the semisimple case, using semisimplicity to conclude the regularized monodromies have finite order

passes.reason: Semisimple quantum multiplication has minimal Jordan/nilpotent complexity and is used as one extreme giving finite-order monodromy.
Semisimplicity is the minimal-Jordan-block/diagonalizable extreme of quantum multiplication, used to derive finite regularized monodromy rather than as a counterexample.
semisimplicity gives the minimal Jordan-block situation for quantum multiplication and hence the strongest finite-order monodromy constraint
Semisimplicity is the diagonalizable/minimal-Jordan-block end of quantum multiplication, used to obtain finite-order monodromy.
Semisimplicity makes all Jordan blocks size one, the minimal-Jordan-block extreme used to force finite regularized monodromy.
Semisimple quantum multiplication has minimal Jordan complexity, with all Jordan blocks of size one, yielding finite-order regularized monodromies.

Used to illustrate that Chen's/Jordan-block bound can force finite regularized monodromy; semisimplicity is the minimal-Jordan-block extreme of quantum multiplication.

examples.name: No-rational-curves extreme
examples.note: The hypothetical case with no rational-curve quantum corrections is used as the opposite extreme from semisimplicity, where c1-multiplication is ordinary cup product and the earlier dimension-type Jordan bound is essentially sharp.
examples.engagement_reason: the no-rational-curves case is invoked as the opposite extreme and facts are stated about the bounds, but no calculation or construction is carried out on it

passes.reason: The no-rational-curves case collapses quantum corrections to the classical product and is explicitly described as an opposite extreme.

examples.name: Jacobian-ring piece of the twisted de Rham complex
examples.note: In answering a question about localization, the speaker points to the top-degree part of the twisted de Rham differential, which computes a Jacobian-type object concentrated on the critical locus, as a concrete analogy for throwing away singular support.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the twisted de Rham/Gauss-Manin complex after inverting q and identifies the top-degree differential as computing the Jacobian-ring piece concentrated on the critical locus
